clinical_note: Established 54-year-old patient seen today at Riverbend Pain Center in Portland, Oregon for follow-up of chronic low back pain. The patient self-reports using medical marijuana obtained from a local dispensary, primarily an evening product taken for sleep, with partial relief of nocturnal pain and improved rest. Dr. Alvarez counseled on starting low and going slow given the variable potency of these products. We discussed that daytime functioning remains limited and reviewed risks of combining this therapy with the current gabapentin regimen. The patient denied any nonmedical use or features of a related use disorder. Plan to continue conservative management and reassess opioid taper in eight weeks.

This is a 67-year-old patient with metastatic colon cancer presenting to Lakeshore Medical Group in Madison, Wisconsin with chemotherapy-induced nausea and poor appetite. After failing ondansetron, NP Chen and I discussed initiating dronabinol (Marinol) 2.5 mg twice daily as an adjunct antiemetic and appetite stimulant. The patient previously tried over-the-counter supplements with minimal benefit and declined inhaled options due to respiratory concerns. We reviewed the sedating effects of this medication and arranged close monitoring. Family expressed understanding of the treatment plan.

Routine annual visit for a 41-year-old patient with fibromyalgia at Maplewood Family Practice in Asheville, North Carolina. The patient reports daily use of a sublingual CBD product purchased online and occasional delta-8 lozenges for breakthrough pain. Dr. Okafor advised caution regarding these unregulated products and the lack of standardized dosing. We discussed that the patient is interested in pursuing a state-licensed program but has not yet obtained a card. Pain remains moderate; plan to add aquatic physical therapy and follow up in three months.

Postoperative day fourteen following total knee arthroplasty for a 60-year-old patient managed by the orthopedic service at Summit Surgical Hospital in Denver, Colorado. The patient disclosed preoperative cannabis use and resumed using a vape pen for pain after discharge. Dr. Reyes counseled on wound healing risks associated with inhaled use and recommended transitioning to a non-inhaled formulation if continued use is desired, while cautioning against high-potency products. Surgical pain is improving and the patient has weaned to acetaminophen during the day. We reinforced that this therapy does not replace prescribed venous thromboembolism prophylaxis.

Initial physical therapy evaluation for chronic neck and shoulder pain in a 36-year-old patient referred to Cedar Hollow Rehabilitation in Boise, Idaho. The patient applies a topical CBD balm before home exercises and reports it reduces local soreness, and also uses a low-dose nightly tincture for sleep. Therapist Daniels documented that the patient avoids smoking and prefers non-inhaled, plant-derived products. We outlined a six-week strengthening program and advised coordinating any changes to these products with the referring physician. Tolerance to manual therapy was good today.

Follow-up after an emergency department visit for an acute migraine in a 29-year-old patient seen at Northgate Community Clinic in Tucson, Arizona. The patient reported using edibles at home before presenting, with a urine drug screen documented as UDS positive for THC as expected. Dr. Patel reviewed that the prior intoxication may have contributed to symptom misattribution and counseled on hydration and trigger avoidance. We discussed that such products are not a reliable abortive for migraine. The patient was started on a triptan and scheduled for neurology referral.

A 48-year-old patient with diabetic peripheral neuropathy returns to Willow Creek Pain Associates in Sacramento, California for medication review. The patient has been prescribed nabiximols (Sativex) oromucosal spray through a clinical trial protocol and reports modest reduction in burning foot pain. NP Sullivan noted no significant intoxication and good adherence to this therapy. We compared the response to the patient's prior use of other inhaled products, which had caused unwanted cognitive effects. Plan to continue the current trial arm and reassess neuropathic scores at the next visit.

Palliative consultation for a 72-year-old patient with pancreatic cancer at Brightwater Cancer Institute in Charleston, South Carolina. The patient experiences refractory nausea and cachexia, and Dr. Nguyen is considering nabilone (Cesamet) given prior intolerance to an earlier antiemetic agent. The patient's daughter reports the family has been giving small amounts of a homemade herbal preparation, which we asked them to pause while we titrate the prescribed therapy. We counseled on the difference between a regulated pharmaceutical product and unregulated over-the-counter alternatives. Comfort-focused goals of care were reaffirmed with the team.

Follow-up for chronic osteoarthritis pain in a 58-year-old patient at Harborview Medical Partners in Providence, Rhode Island. The patient successfully reduced ibuprofen use after starting a medical cannabis regimen, using a lower-potency daytime preparation and a small evening dose. Dr. Alvarez reviewed the patient's purchase log and reinforced safe storage away from grandchildren. We noted the patient declined a stronger flower product to avoid impairment while driving and screened negative for misuse. Blood pressure and renal function remain stable; continue current plan.

New patient evaluation for chronic pelvic pain in a 33-year-old patient referred to Stonebridge Pain & Spine in Minneapolis, Minnesota. The patient reports prior recreational cannabis use that they have been actively declining over the past year in favor of mindfulness and a low-dose plant-based capsule. Dr. Kim documented that the patient is hesitant about psychoactive products due to a history of anxiety, with no evidence of a use disorder. We discussed the more favorable side-effect profile of the non-intoxicating option and the limited evidence for cannabinoids in pelvic pain. A multidisciplinary plan including pelvic floor therapy was initiated.

Six-month follow-up for a 9-year-old patient with Dravet syndrome managed at Glenford Children's Hospital in Columbus, Ohio. The patient continues Epidiolex oral solution with a meaningful reduction in seizure frequency reported by the parents. Dr. Marsh reviewed liver function tests, which remain within normal limits, and confirmed no use of any unregulated products from outside sources. The family was counseled that this pharmaceutical-grade cannabidiol differs substantially from over-the-counter store-bought formulations. Dosing was adjusted for weight gain and follow-up labs were ordered through Glenford Children's Hospital.

Discharge summary from a twelve-visit course of therapy for lumbar radiculopathy in a 45-year-old patient at Foothills Sports Medicine in Salt Lake City, Utah. The patient reported using a topical pain cream and vaping a high-potency concentrate for flare-ups during the program. Therapist Brooks noted improved functional scores and encouraged tapering reliance on inhaled products as activity tolerance increased. We documented the patient's interest in switching to a non-inhaled formulation and referred this discussion back to Dr. Alvarez. Home exercise program reviewed and patient verbalized understanding.

Post-discharge follow-up for a 51-year-old patient evaluated for chest tightness, ultimately deemed musculoskeletal, at Rosewood Urgent Care in Albuquerque, New Mexico. The patient reported heavy daily use of delta-9 products purchased locally, with a urine drug screen positive for the relevant metabolites. NP Chen counseled on the cyclic vomiting and cardiovascular risks associated with high-dose use, and noted features consistent with cannabis use disorder. We discussed gradually decreasing intake and provided resources for cessation support. The patient agreed to follow up with primary care and consider alternative pain strategies.

Routine visit for a 63-year-old patient with chronic low back pain and prior lumbar fusion at Riverbend Pain Center in Portland, Oregon. The patient holds a state MMJ card and uses a balanced oral product for symptom control, having discontinued smoking hashish several years ago. Dr. Reyes reviewed the urine drug screen, which showed expected results, and counseled on continued avoidance of combustible products. We noted the patient declined an increase in opioid dosing, preferring to rely on the current regimen and physical activity. Functional goals were updated and the next follow-up scheduled in four months.

Annual wellness visit for a 39-year-old patient with tension headaches and insomnia at Lakeshore Medical Group in Madison, Wisconsin. The patient has been trialing an over-the-counter hemp oil sleep aid along with a CBN sleep product purchased locally, reporting mild benefit for sleep onset. Dr. Okafor counseled on the limited regulation and inconsistent labeling of these unregulated supplements and recommended documenting any exposure before upcoming employment screening. The patient is curious about formal certification for plant-based therapy but plans to first trial sleep hygiene measures and a non-intoxicating regimen. We scheduled a follow-up to reassess in two months.
